[JS Weird World] No.33 Quick Reminder: The Syntax Parser
We already talked about this topic way back, but I want to bring it up one more time to really drive the idea home — because the stuff coming up next is all going to be tied to the

Remember: the code we write doesn't get run by the computer directly. There's a middleman program sitting between our code and the computer, translating it into instructions the computer can actually understand.

That compiler has a ton of features and moving parts inside it, and one of them is the thing we're talking about here — the syntax parser. It reads through your code to check whether it's valid. For example, it goes character by character to see if your code makes sense —
becomes r-e-t-u-r-n. If somewhere in the middle it turns into r-e-t-r..., the syntax parser has no idea what you're saying, so keep that rule in mind.
Otherwise you're going to be seeing this guy a lot:
Ah, ah, ah! You made a typo!

We touched on this topic way back, but it's worth bringing up again to reinforce the idea — because the stuff coming up next is all tied to the syntax parser. This post is a quick reminder of what the syntax parser actually does and why it matters.
